21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.

51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
75	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
76	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
77	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
78	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
79	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
80	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
81	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
82	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
83	% assertion."

111	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
112	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
113	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
114	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
115	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
116	
117	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
118	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
119	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
120	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
121	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
122	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
123	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
124	
125	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
126	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
127	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
128	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
129	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
130	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
131	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
132	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
133	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
134	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

352	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single

446	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
447	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.

449	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
450	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the

452	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne

456	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no

494	\begin{proposition}

503	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
504	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
505	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
506	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
507	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
508	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
509	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in

511	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
512	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
513	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
514	\end{proposition}
515	
516	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
517	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
518	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
519	\begin{proof}
520	  \uses{def:genus}
521	  \leanok
522	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
523	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
524	
525	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
526	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
527	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
528	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
529	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
530	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
531	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
532	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
533	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
534	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
535	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
536	\end{proof}

550	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
551	
552	\begin{theorem}
553	
554	\leanok
555	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
556	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
557	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
559	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
560	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
561	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
562	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
563	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
564	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
565	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
566	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
567	
568	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
569	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
570	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
571	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
572	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
573	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
574	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
575	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
576	  \eta[A]$.
577	\end{theorem}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
581	  \leanok
582	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
583	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
584	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
585	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
586	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
587	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
588	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
589	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
590	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
591	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
592	
593	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
594	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
595	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
596	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
597	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
598	\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
  Let $W$, $Z$ be schemes with $W$ \emph{reduced}, $W$ a \emph{Jacobson space}, and $Z$
  \emph{separated}. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z$ be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
  $x \in W$, the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
  $\Spec \kappa(x) \to W$ (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$), then $g_1 = g_2$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  This is the ``dense closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
  dominant probe.

  Form the coproduct $P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)$ and the morphism
  $\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W$ defined by $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$ of the residue-field points
  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$. Its topological range contains every closed point of $W$:
  the inclusion $\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P$ composes with $\mathrm{probe}$ to
  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ (the defining property of $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$), and the
  range of $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ is the singleton $\{x\}$
  ($\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}$). Because $W$ is a Jacobson space, the closed points
  are dense ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W$), so
  the range of $\mathrm{probe}$ is dense and $\mathrm{probe}$ is \emph{dominant}.

  Componentwise, $g_1$ and $g_2$ agree after $\mathrm{probe}$: precomposing with each coproduct
  inclusion $\iota_x$ reduces (by $\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}$) to
  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$,
  which is the hypothesis at the closed point $x$. By the universal property of the coproduct
  ($\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}$) this gives $\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2$.
  Since $\mathrm{probe}$ is dominant and $Z$ is separated, $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ cancels it
  on the left to yield $g_1 = g_2$.
\end{proof}

  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$, a $p_2$-saturated open $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ mapping into an
  affine open $U_0 \subseteq Z$ --- fix a \emph{closed} point $x$ of $U$. Then $f$ and the collapsed
  map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$, i.e.\ $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$) agree at $x$
  after the residue-field probe $\Spec \kappa(x) \to U$:
  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
  proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
  deliberately avoided. The closed point $x$ of $U$ lies over a closed point
  $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$; since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type
  over $\bar k$, the residue field is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Saturation $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$ inside $U$, so $f$ maps $X_y$
  into the affine $U_0$. Because $X_y$ is proper integral over $\bar k$, its global sections form a
  field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), module-finite over $\bar k$
  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$); algebraic closedness collapses the finite
  extension, giving $\Gamma(X_y, \mathcal O) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by
  its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so $f|_{X_y}$ factors through a
  single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$. That point is $f(x_0, y)$, since the $\bar k$-point $(x_0, y)$ lies
  on $X_y$; and $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$ by the definition of $\mathtt{retract}$.
  Evaluating at the residue-field probe of the closed point $x$ gives the asserted equality. This
  per-slice statement is the chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}; its globalisation
  over the dense closed points is \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}.
\end{proof}
